perturbation.original_text: 13. For purposes of this Agreement, “ Person ” shall be broadly interpreted to include any individual, corporation, company, partnership, limited liability company, trust or other group or entity (including any court, government or agency, commission, board or authority thereof, federal, state or local, domestic, foreign or multinational).
perturbation.changed_text: 13. For purposes of this Agreement, “ Person ” shall be narrowly interpreted to include only natural persons, explicitly excluding corporations, companies, partnerships, limited liability companies, trusts or other groups or entities.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Person' is altered to exclude legal entities, which contradicts standard legal interpretations and could affect enforcement of the agreement against organizations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1, which states that the word 'person' includes corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals, unless the context indicates otherwise. The modified text specifically excludes these entities, creating a direct contradiction.

Words denoting number, gender, and so forth In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise- words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words "insane" and "insane person
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 1 U.S. Code § 1 by narrowly defining 'Person' to exclude corporations, companies, partnerships, limited liability companies, and trusts, whereas the statute states that the word 'person' includes these entities unless the context indicates otherwise. The scraped law snippet 1, referring to 'Words denoting number, gender, and so forth' in relation to the meaning of any Act of Congress, supports this by stating that words importing the singular include and apply to several persons, parties, or things, implying a broader definition of 'person' than the one provided in the changed text, which is in direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The term “Confidential Information” does not include any information which (a) at the time of disclosure or thereafter is generally available to or known by the public (other than as a result of its disclosure by you or your Representatives in breach of this Agreement), (b) was available to you or your Representatives on a non-confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Company, (c) becomes available to you or your Representatives on a non-confidential basis from a Person who is not known by you or your Representatives (as applicable) to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Company, or is not otherwise prohibited from transmitting the information to you or your Representatives (as applicable), or (d) is or was independently developed by you or your Representatives without reference to, incorporation of, or other use of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The term “Confidential Information” does not include any information, regardless of its public availability, prior knowledge, or independent development. All information disclosed is considered non-confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the exclusions for publicly available or independently developed information, effectively nullifying the concept of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the definition of 'trade secret' under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S. Code § 1839), which requires the information to be secret and have commercial value from not being generally known. By stating that all information is non-confidential regardless of its actual secrecy, the contract language directly opposes this requirement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) by stating that all information is non-confidential, regardless of its secrecy or independent development. This directly opposes the definition of 'trade secret' in 18 U.S. Code § 1839, which requires the information to be secret and have commercial value from not being generally known. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'trade secret' and states that it derives 'independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known'. By removing the exclusions for publicly available or independently developed information, the modified contract makes it impossible for any information to qualify as a trade secret under the DTSA, thus creating a direct contradiction. The original contract had carveouts that allowed certain types of data to be classified as confidential, which aligns with the legal definition; the changed contract explicitly voids any such classification.

perturbation.original_text: 1. You hereby agree that the Confidential Information will be used solely for the purpose of evaluating and implementing the Transaction and that such information will be kept confidential by you; provided that any such information may be disclosed to your officers, directors, employees, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, financial advisors, consultants, other agents or representatives and financing sources (such Persons hereinafter collectively being referred to as your “ Representatives ” who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating and implementing the Transaction, have been informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information, and have been advised that such information is to be kept confidential, and provided , further , that such officers, directors, employees, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, financial advisors, consultants, other agents or representatives and financing sources shall not be deemed to be your Representatives unless (but solely to the extent that) you furnish Confidential Information to them. You agree you will be liable for any breaches of the terms of this Agreement by such Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. You hereby agree that the Confidential Information will be used solely for the purpose of evaluating and implementing the Transaction and that such information will be kept confidential by you; provided that any such information may be disclosed to any individual, regardless of their relationship to you or their need to know. You agree you will not be liable for any breaches of the terms of this Agreement by any individuals to whom the information is disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the restriction on who can receive confidential information and eliminates liability for breaches by those individuals, undermining the confidentiality agreement's core purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the duty to maintain secrecy required to protect trade secrets under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act § 4, which outlines the elements for misappropriation, including improper disclosure or use of a trade secret. By allowing disclosure to anyone without consequence, the contract undermines the protection afforded by the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of maintaining confidentiality, which is a key aspect of trade secret protection. The original text limits disclosure to specific representatives with a need to know and holds the discloser liable for breaches by those representatives. The changed text removes these limitations, allowing disclosure to anyone without consequence. While no specific snippet is provided, the Law Explanation indicates the contradiction arises with the duty to maintain secrecy to protect trade secrets under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act § 4. By eliminating the restriction on who can receive confidential information and removing liability for breaches, the changed text significantly weakens trade secret protection and creates a legal contradiction. The USLegal snippet provided does refer to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, stating it provides states with a legal framework for improved trade secret protection to industry, further supporting that the changed text weakens that protection.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to conflicts-of-law principles. Each party hereto irrevocably and unconditionally (a) consents to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of New York and of the United States of America located in the State of New York for any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this agreement (and irrevocably and unconditionally agrees not to commence any such action, suit, or proceeding except in such courts), (b) waives any objection to the laying of venue of any such action, suit or proceeding in any such courts and (c) waives and agrees not to plead or claim that any such action, suit or proceeding brought in any such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Korea, without regard to conflicts-of-law principles. Each party hereto irrevocably and unconditionally (a) consents to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of New York and of the United States of America located in the State of New York for any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this agreement (and irrevocably and unconditionally agrees not to commence any such action, suit, or proceeding except in such courts), (b) waives any objection to the laying of venue of any such action, suit or proceeding in any such courts and (c) waives and agrees not to plead or claim that any such action, suit or proceeding brought in any such court has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: Attempting to apply North Korean law to a contract adjudicated in New York courts creates a conflict because the legal systems are incompatible and North Korean law may be unenforceable in the U.S.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_citation: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York generally respects choice-of-law provisions, there are limitations. Applying North Korean law may be against public policy or unenforceable due to practical reasons, conflicting with the principle that choice of law should lead to a reasonable and applicable legal framework for resolving disputes. Choice of law provisions are generally acceptable, but cannot contravene public policy. It's highly unlikely that a U.S. court would enforce North Korean law.

Claim for contribution. Except as provided in sections 15-108 and 18-201 of the general obligations law, sections eleven and twenty-nine of the workers' compensation law, or the workers' compensation law of any other state or the federal government, two or more persons who are subject to liability for damages for the same personal injury, injury to property or wrongful death, may claim contribution among them whether or not an action has been brought or a judgment has been rendered against the person from whom contribution is sought.
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that New York law governs the agreement. The changed text replaces this with North Korean law. The Law Explanation indicates that while choice of law provisions are generally acceptable, they cannot contravene public policy. Applying North Korean law to a contract adjudicated in New York courts would likely be against public policy and unenforceable, as detailed in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. This creates a direct contradiction because the legal systems are incompatible and enforcement of North Korean law within a New York court is highly improbable. The provided snippets are not directly relevant, but the general principle that choice of law must be reasonable and applicable, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, supports the conclusion that applying North Korean law would be contradictory.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and our current discussions, the Company may be irreparably damaged by any unauthorized disclosure of any Confidential Information and you and the Company may be irreparably damaged by any unauthorized disclosure of our discussions or by any breach of this Agreement by you, the Company or the Representatives of you and the Company. Without prejudice to other rights and remedies otherwise available, you and the Company therefore agree that the other party hereto shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including an injunction or specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement by the other party hereto or the Representatives of the other party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Given the nature of the Confidential Information and our current discussions, the Company may be irreparably damaged by any unauthorized disclosure of any Confidential Information and you and the Company may be irreparably damaged by any unauthorized disclosure of our discussions or by any breach of this Agreement by you, the Company or the Representatives of you and the Company. Without prejudice to other rights and remedies otherwise available, you and the Company therefore agree that the other party hereto shall be entitled to seek monetary damages only, and specifically waives the right to seek equitable relief, including an injunction or specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement by the other party hereto or the Representatives of the other party hereto.
perturbation.explanation: This modification prevents the injured party from seeking an injunction, which is a common remedy for breach of confidentiality agreements. This could be seen as limiting remedies that a court might otherwise grant.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the general principles regarding equitable remedies, as articulated in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359. While parties can contractually limit remedies, a complete waiver of equitable relief, particularly when monetary damages are inadequate to compensate for the breach (as often is the case with confidential information), could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, potentially rendering the waiver unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Restatement (Second) of Contracts - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Widely cited American legal treatise The Restatement (Second) of the Law of Contracts is a legal treatise from the second series of the Restatements of the Law , and seeks to inform judges and lawyers about general principles of contract common law. It is one of the best-recognized and frequently cited legal treatises [ 1 ] in all of American jurisprudence . Every first-year law student in the United States is exposed to it, and it is a frequently cited non-binding authority in all of U.S. common law in the areas of contracts and commercial transactions. [ 2 ] The American Law Institute began work on the second edition in 1962 and completed it in 1979; the version in use at present has a copyright year of 1981. [ 3 ] For an explanation of the purpose of a restatement of law, see Restatement of the Law . Use and commentary [ edit ] Legal scholars and jurists have commented extensively on the Restatement, both in contrasting it with aspects of the first Restatement, and in evaluating its influence and effectiveness in reaching its stated objectives. It is in this context of direct review that one can find numerous arguments both favoring and criticizing some aspects of the Restatement as an independent source of legal scholarship. [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] Although several sections of the Restatement contained new rules which sometimes contradicted existing law, courts citing these sections have predominantly adopted the Restatement's view, citing them as a court would cite statute or code. [ 9 ] Far more common, however, is the practice of citing the Restatement to clarify generally accepted doctrine in every major area of contract and commercial law. It is in this context of legal research that one can find the Restatement used as direct substantiation and persuasive authority , to validate the arguments and interpretations of individual legal practitioners. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Legacy [ edit ] Although the Restatement of Contracts is still an influential academic work, certain aspects have been superseded in everyday legal practice by the Uniform Commercial Code . Specifically, the UCC has replaced the Restatement (Second) of Contracts in regard to the sale of goods. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts remains the unofficial authority for aspects of contract law which find their genesis in the common law principles of the United States and, previously, England. [ citation needed ] See also [ edit ] Arthur Linton Corbin , first reporter Corpus Juris Secundum References [ edit ] ^ Acceptable citation format under the Bluebook : "Restatement (Second) of Contracts § ___ (Am. L. Inst. 1981)." The Bluebook: A Uniform System of Citation § 12.8.5 (Columbia Law Review Ass'n et al. eds., 17th ed. 2000) ^ Bauman, Richard (2002). Ideology and Community in the First Wave of Critical Legal Studies . Toronto: University of Toronto Press. ISBN 0-8020-8341-2 . ^ Beale, Hugh (2002). Contract Law: Casebooks for the Common Law of Europe . Oxford: Hart Pub. ISBN 978-1-84113-237-2 . ^ Interpretation and Legal Effect in the Second "Restatement of Contracts" Robert Braucher Columbia Law Review , Vol. 81, No. 1 (Jan., 1981), pp. 13-18 ^ Jacobs, Legal Realism or Legal Fiction? Impracticability Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts , 87 Com. L.J. 289 (1982). ^ Braucher, Offer and Acceptance in the Second Restatement , 74 Yale L.J. 302 (1964). ^ Kelly, The Codification of Contract Law in the Twentieth Century , 88 Dick. L. Rev. 289 (1984). ^ Keyes, The Restatement (Second): Its Misleading Quality and a Proposal for Its Amelioration , 13 Pepp. L. Rev. 23 (1985). ^ Gregory E. Maggs, Ipse Dixit: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts and the Modern Development of Contract Law , 66 Geo. Wash. L. Rev. 508 (1998). ^ Restitution in the Second "Restatement of Contracts"Joseph M. Perillo Columbia Law Review , Vol. 81, No. 1 (Jan., 1981), pp. 37-51 ^ Contracts:
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement by waiving the right to seek equitable relief, including injunctions or specific performance, and instead limiting the remedy to monetary damages only. The Law Explanation states that while parties can limit remedies, a complete waiver of equitable relief, especially where monetary damages are inadequate, could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which addresses general principles of contract common law and is often cited in American jurisprudence, suggesting that the altered clause's enforceability is questionable under common law principles, given the context of confidentiality agreements where monetary damages might be insufficient.
